content.full_text:
Re
Fern PolrrIcal PnecrrcEs CourrrrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-56@ o Fax (916) 322-0886
March 9,2A04
Tei Yukimoto, Deputy City Attomey
City of Fresno - City Hall
2600 Fresno Street
Fresno, CA9372l-3602
Your Request for fnformal Assistance
Our FiIe No. I-03-300
Dear Ms. Yukimoto:
This letter is in response to your request on behalf of Jerry Duncan,
Councilmember for the City of Fresno, for advice regarding the conflict of interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act').I Becausewe lack specific
information on the trust instrument creating one ofthe council member's economic
interests, on the size of the trust itsel{, its investrnent in Comcast, and on the terms of the
current franchise agreement and aqy amendments that may be proposed, we can provide
you only with informal assistance.'
QUESTION
Does Councilmember Duncan have a conflict of interest disqualifying him from
participating in negotiations for renewal of the city's franchise agreement with Comcast?
CONCLUSION
Councilmember Duncan will have a disquali$ring conflict of interest if it is
reasonably foreseeable that a decision on renewal of this agreement will have a material
financial effect on Comcast, the trust, or on the council member's personal finances. We
do not have all the information necessary to answer this question conclusively, but we
outline the analytical process whereby Councilmember Duncan may reach the conclusion
appropriate under all the circumstances.
I Government Code sections 81000 - gl}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18-997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not "orf", 
the immunity provided by a Commission opinion or formal
written advice. (Regulation I 8329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)
'ffii;a-r


File No. I-03-300
Page No. 2
FACTS
The City of Fresno is currently negotiating with Comcast for renewal of the cable
television franchise agreement, pursuant to the local cable television ordinance. As you
explained in a telephone conversation, Councilmember Duncan is a beneficiary of his late
father's kust, and his wife is paid $500 per month for administering a portion of the trust.
Although hg himself does not presently receive any income from the trust, he has a 50
percent non-revocable interest in the principal of the trust upon dissolution. A portion of
the trust assets are being invested by Union Bank, including a S35,000 invesfinent in
Comcast. Councilmember Duncan has an $8,000 invesfinent in Comcast stock, in
addition to the trust holdings. He would like to participate in the negotiations for renewal
of the Comcast franchise agreeme,nt. The city council consists of seven elected members
who will decide whether or not to renew the agreement with Comcast.
ANALYSIS
Your question refers to the Act's conflict of interest rules. Section 87100
prohibits a public official from making participating in making; or othenrise using his
or her official position to influence a govemmelrtal decision in which the official has a
financial interest. The Commission has adopted a standard eight-step analysis for
deciding whether an official has a disqualiffing conflict of interest (regulation 18700,
subdivisions OXI) - (8)), which we apply to the circumstances you have described.
Steps l. & 2. Is Councilmember Duncan a public oflicial who will be
making participating in making or (influencingo a govemmental decision?
The conflict of interest prohibition applies only to public officials. As an elected
mernber of the city council, I[r. Duncan is a public official. (Section 82048; regulation
18701(a).) By partictpating in negotiations on the renewal of the Comcast franchise,
Councilmember Duncan would make, participate in making, and/or be using his official
position to influence govemmental decisions. (Section 87100; regulations 18702.1-
187023)3
3 If a public official's office is listed in section 87200 (*872A0 filers" include city council
members and members ofplanning cornmissions) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision
noticed at a public meeting, the official musfl (l) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, verbally
identiff each type of economic interest involved in the decision as well as details oftle economic interest,
as discussed inregulation 18702.50)(1)(8), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and
(3) leave the room for the duration ofthe discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent
calendars, absences and speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules
found in regulation 18702.5, subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.) Since Councilmember
Duncan is a member of the city council (a position enumerated in section 57200) these requirements would
applyto him.


File No. I-03-300
Page No. 3
Step 3. What are Councilmember Duncan's economic interests?
The economic interests that might give rise to a conflict of interest are defined in
section 87 1 03 and regulations I 8703- I 8703.5. Because his wife receives $500 per month
from the trust in compe,nsation forher administrative senrices, CouncilmemberDuncan
has an interest in the trust as a source of income. (Section 87103(c); regulation
187033)4
Most trusts are business entities under the Act. If the tnrst is a business entity,
it would be an economic interest under section 87103(a) and (d). You characterize
Councilmember Duncan's trust as a "family kust." The chief difference between a
"business trusf' and the typical "family trust" is found in the purpose of the trust. The
primary purpose of a business trust is to carry on a business enterprise for profi! while
the primary purpose of the tpical family trust is to conser:re the properfy of the trust for
the beneficiaries, with incidental powers of investrnent and sale. (See, e.g., the Auran
Advice Letter, No. A-01-138.) The tlpical family trust is not treated as a business entity
under the Act. So long as it is not properly regarded as a "business trust," it is not an
economic interest under section 87103(a) and (d). Forpurposes ofthis letter, we will
asfllme that the tnrst is not a "business trust-"
Under certain circumstances the property, in@me, and investnents of any trust
are included among a public official's economic interests. Regulation 18234 describes
these circumstances as follows:
"(a) An official has an economic interest in thepro rata share ofthe
interests in real property, sources of incomg and investnents of a tnrst in
which the official has a direct, indirect, orbeneficial interest of l0 percent
or greatetr "
However, even when the official's interest in a trust is 10 percent or greater,
regulation 18234limits the circumstances under which the beneficiary of a trust will be
considered to have an economic interest in his pro rata share of the trust's real property,
investrnents and in sources of income to the tust. (sullivan Advice Letter, No. A-95-
234.) T\e rule pertinent to Councilme,rrrber Duncan's circumstances will be fognd in
regulation lSna@)Q), which specifies that an official has an economic interest in the
trust assets when the official is:
*Q) Abeneficiary and:
(A) Presently receives income; or
(B) Has an irrevocable future right to receive income or principal. For
purposes of this subsection, an individual has an irrevocable future right to
receive income or principal if the trust is irrevocable, unless one of the
following applies:
o The Act defines "income" to include a communityproperty interest in the income of a spouse. (Section
82030.) Thus, so long as Councilmember Duncan's wife has earned $1,000 or more from any single source
of income within the preceding 12 months, it is a source of income to Councilmember Duncan as well.


File No. I-03-300
Page No. 4
(i) Powers exist to consume, invade, or appoint the principal forthe
benefit of beneficiaries other than the official and such powerc are not
limited by an ascertainable standard relating to the health, education,
sppofr, or maintenance of the beneficiaries; or
(ii) Under the terms of the trust, someone other than the official can
designate the persons who shall possess or enjoythe trust property or
income."
Councilmember Duncan is a beneficiary of the tnrst, from which his spouse is
currently receiving $500 in monthly income. As you advised us ov€r the telephone,
Councilme,mber Duncan's interest in the tnrst is irrevocablg and cannot be eroded in the
manner described in this regulation. Therefore Counsilmember Duncan has a pro-rata
economic interest in the investments ofthe trust, including its investnent in Comcast,
which he values at $35,000. Since his 5O-percent share in the Comcast investnent is
$17,500, and he holds an additional $8,000 in Comcast stock, Councilmernber Duncan
has an economic interest in Comcasf as well as in the trust.
Finally,like all public officials CouncilmemberDuncan has an economic interest
in his personal finances, including those of his immediate family. (Section 87fi3;
regulation 18703.5.) Since you have not described any other economic interests, the
following analysis is limited to the three interests just mentioned.
Step 4. Are these economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the
decisions?
Business entities like Comcast are directly involved in a decision before an
official's agerrcy when the business entity:
"(l) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject o{, the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the subject
of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval,
denial or revocation of any lice,lrse, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the subject person." (Regulation lS70a.1(a).)
Comcast would therefore be directly involved in any governmental decision
touching the renewal of its cable television contract. In similar fashion, a public official
is deemed to be directly involved in a decision that has any financial effect on his
personal finances or those ofhis immediate family. (Regulation 18704.5.) The trust,
however, is not directly involved in decisions on renewal of the franchise agreement.


File No. I-03-300
Page No. 5
Steps 5. & 6. What are the applicable materiality standards, and is it
reasonably foreseeable that the financial effects of the governmental decisions upon
the official's economic interests will be material?
A conflict of interest may arise onlywhen the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interests is material. (Regulation
18700(a).) Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on an economic interest will be material, depending on the nature of the
economic interest and whether that interest is directly or indirectly involved in the
agency's decision. For an investment interest in a business emtity like Comcast, which is
directly involved in a governmental decision, the materiality standard is provided at
regulation 1 8705. I (b):
"(b) Directly involved business entities.
(1) General Rule: Unless the exception in subdivision
OX2) ofthis regulation applies, the financial effects of a
govemme,ntal decision on a business entity which is
directly involved in the gove,mmental decision is presrnnd
to be material. This presumption maybe rebutted byproof
that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the governme,lrtal
decision will have any financial effect on the business
entity.
(2) Exception: If the public official's only economic interest
in the business entity is an investnent interest (see
Governmexlt Code section 87103(a)), and the public
official's investrnent in the business entity is worth $25,000
or less, then apply the materiality standarrds in subdivision
(cXl) of this regulation if the business entity is listed on the
Fortune 50O, or the materiality standards in zuMivision
(c)(2) of this regulation if the business entity is listed on the
New York Stock Exchange, or if not listed on the New
York Stock Exchange, for its most recent fiscal year had
earnings before taxes of no less than:
(A) $Z.S million, or
@) such other amount described at Rule 102.01C ofthe
New York Stock Exchange's Listed CompanyManual (or
any superseding rule ofthe New York Stock Exchange
describing its financial standards for initial listing)."
It appears that Councilmember Duncan's investrnent in Comcast, combining his
personal holdings of $8,000 and his pro-rata share of the $35,000 investment held for his
benefit by the trust, amounts to $25,500. If the value of these investrnents exceeds
$25,000 at the time of any governmental decision, Councilmember Duncan will not be
able to take advantage of the exception provided at regulation 18705.1(bX2), and any
financial effect on ComcastwlTlbepresumed to be material. If the current value of his
investrnent falls to $25,000 or less at the time of any decision, Councilmember Duncan


File No. I-03-300
Page No. 6
will have to determine whether the foreseeable financial effect of that desision meets or
exceeds the materiality threshold specified in subdivision (b)(2) for investments worth
$25,000 or less.
The materiality standard applicable to an indirectly involved nonprofit entity (the
family trust) is given in regulation 18705.3(b). The specific materiality standard would
be that described under either regulation 1S705.3(b)(2XE) - if the annual income of the
trustis greatu than$100,000butlessthan$1,000,000-or18705.3OX2XDifthetrust's
annual income is less than $100,000. With knowledge of the trust's financial size,
Councilmember Duncan may dete,r:nine which of these materiality standards would apply
to the trust-s
As to personal financial effects, regulation 18705.5(a) provides the materiality
standard pertinent to the circumstances you describe:
"(a) A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a
public official's personal finances is material if it is at least
$250 in arry l2-month period. When determining whether
a govemmental decision has amaterial financial effect on a
public official's economic interest in his orherpersonal
finances, neither a financial effect on the value of real
property owned directly or indirectly by the official, nor a
financial effect on the gross revenues, expexrses, or value of
assets and liabilities of a business entity in which the
official has a direct or indirect investnent interest shall be
considered-"
We next turn to the question of foreseeabilty. A material financial effect is
reasonably foreseeable, within the meaning of section 871A3, if it is "substamtially likely''
that such an effect will result from the decision. (Regulation 18706; In re Thorner (l9l1)
I FPPC Ops. 198.) A financial effect need not be a certainty to be considered reasonably
foreseeable. But if it is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. Qn re
Thorner, supra.\
If the value of Councilmember Duncan's invesfinent in Comcast is greater than
$25,000 at the time of any grven decision concerning renewal of the cable television
franchise, any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on Comcast (positive or negative)
will be presumed to be material. On the other hand, if the value of Councilmember
Duncan's investrnent is $25,000 or less, he will have to decide whether it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a financial effect on Comcast in the amount stated
in regulation 18705.1(b)(2), (c)(1), or (c)(2).
5 Of course, if it appears that the family trust should be classified as a 'business trust," apply
regulation 18705.1(c), as appropriate to the financial size ofthe business.


File No. I-03-300
Page No. 7
The process is the same for determining whether the decision will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on Councilmember Duncan's personal
finances or on the trust, as measured against the materiality standards applicable to these
economic interests, as discussed above.
Steps 7. & 8. "Public Generally" and *Legally Required Participation,
Even when it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision will have a material
financial effect on a public official's economic interest, there is no conflict of interest
when the financial effect on the official is not distinguishable from effects on the public
generally. (Section 83 I 12; regulation I 87 07 .) We have no information suggesting that
an effect ofthe decision(s) in question on the council member's economic interests would
be indistinguishable from effects on the public generally.
Step eight is an exception to the general requirement of disqualification from
decisionmaking whe,n an official has a conflict of interest. This exception applies when
the official's participation in the decision is "legally required," as defined at section
87101 and regulation 18708. This exception is quite narow, and we do not have any
reason to believe that it might be applicable in Councilmember Duncan's circrmrstances,
should he find that he has a conflict of interest in decisions on the Comcast franchise.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By: Lr.4,Ub
Lawrence T. Woodlock 
A-
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
Enclosure
LTwjg

The process is the same for determining whether the decision will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on Councilmember Duncan's personal
finances or on the trust, as measured against the materiality standards applicable to these
economic interests, as discussed above.
Steps 7. & 8. "Public Generally" and *Legally Required Participation,
Even when it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision will have a material
financial effect on a public official's economic interest, there is no conflict of interest
when the financial effect on the official is not distinguishable from effects on the public
generally. (Section 83 I 12; regulation I 87 07 .) We have no information suggesting that
an effect ofthe decision(s) in question on the council member's economic interests would
be indistinguishable from effects on the public generally.
Step eight is an exception to the general requirement of disqualification from
decisionmaking whe,n an official has a conflict of interest. This exception applies when
the official's participation in the decision is "legally required," as defined at section
87101 and regulation 18708. This exception is quite narow, and we do not have any
reason to believe that it might be applicable in Councilmember Duncan's circrmrstances,
should he find that he has a conflict of interest in decisions on the Comcast franchise.

QUESTION Does Councilmember Duncan have a conflict of interest disqualifying him from participating in negotiations for renewal of the city's franchise agreement with Comcast? CONCLUSION Councilmember Duncan will have a disquali$ring conflict of interest if it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision on renewal of this agreement will have a material financial effect on Comcast, the trust, or on the council member's personal finances. We do not have all the information necessary to answer this question conclusively, but we outline the analytical process whereby Councilmember Duncan may reach the conclusion appropriate under all the circumstances. (Regulation I 8329(c)(3), copy enclosed.) 'ffii;a-r File No. I-03-300 Page No. 2 FACTS The City of Fresno is currently negotiating with Comcast for renewal of the cable television franchise agreement, pursuant to the local cable television ordinance. As you explained in a telephone conversation, Councilmember Duncan is a beneficiary of his late father's kust, and his wife is paid $500 per month for administering a portion of the trust. Although hg himself does not presently receive any income from the trust, he has a 50 percent non-revocable interest in the principal of the trust upon dissolution. A portion of the trust assets are being invested by Union Bank, including a S35,000 invesfinent in Comcast. Councilmember Duncan has an $8,000 invesfinent in Comcast stock, in addition to the trust holdings. He would like to participate in the negotiations for renewal of the Comcast franchise agreeme,nt. The city council consists of seven elected members who will decide whether or not to renew the agreement with Comcast. ANALYSIS Your question refers to the Act's conflict of interest rules. Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making participating in making; or othenrise using his or her official position to influence a govemmelrtal decision in which the official has a financial interest. The Commission has adopted a standard eight-step analysis for deciding whether an official has a disqualiffing conflict of interest (regulation 18700, subdivisions OXI) - (8)),
